questions.text: I hold the view that my low reactivity means I'm able to stay with difficult realities without flinching.
I find that I contribute most when I maintain an objective stance rather than allowing myself to be moved by the emotional gravity of a situation.
I notice when a person's typing speed in a live chat changes in response to the emotional content of the discussion.
I tend to accept invitations at face value without wondering if there is a subtext.
I feel that the moral weight I assign to suffering is not an opinion I hold but a conviction that organizes my priorities.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering, once I have truly registered it, cannot be set aside without a cost to my integrity.
I tend to miss the social signal when someone says 'we should do this sometime' without ever intending to follow through.
I hold the view that taking care of myself first puts me in the best position to live well.
I hold the view that emotional detachment during painful situations is not a lack of caring but a different and sometimes superior way of caring.
I notice that my jaw clenches and my shoulders tense the instant I feel threatened or upset.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is what transforms ethical knowledge into ethical practice.
I evaluate options holistically in the moment rather than against predetermined criteria.
I accept that last-minute rushes are part of my natural rhythm and often produce my best work.
I feel most effective when I can direct the allocation of responsibilities.
I hold the view that the most useful thing I can offer in a painful situation is clear, structured thinking rather than an emotional response.
I routinely reduce my own workload expectations so I can pitch in where others are struggling.
I pick up on the way people position themselves physically relative to those they trust.
I believe my knowledge is wasted if it only benefits me.
My body temperature seems to fluctuate with my emotional state — hot when agitated, cold when frightened.
I establish personal policies for recurring decisions so I don't have to deliberate each time.
I would share a professional secret or competitive advantage if it would significantly help someone else.
I prepare talking points before important conversations.
I feel that flat, collaborative structures produce better results.
I often find that I am the one defining what success looks like.
I notice that my appetite and energy fluctuate dramatically with my emotional state.
I often champion a particular direction and rally others behind it.
I can tell when someone responds to a text with a shorter message than usual and what that brevity means.
I find that traveling alone to pursue my own interests is more fulfilling than compromising on a group itinerary.
I prefer that new initiatives come from a collective mandate.
I notice that I rarely need to actively calm myself down — my system does it automatically.
I do not naturally sense when a room is tense.
I organize information I consume into notes, highlights, or summaries for later use.
I return things when I remember, without feeling pressured by a specific timeline.
I consider my ability to amplify others' voices an important professional asset.
I find that my emotional thermostat has a narrow range — I don't often reach the extremes.
I believe my personal fulfillment should not depend on what I do for other people.
